description: The six-man council appointed by the dying Umar selects Uthman ibn Affan as the third Caliph of Islam.

figures.name: Uthman ibn Affan
figures.role: elected third Caliph

figures.name: Ali ibn Abi Talib
figures.role: candidate; narrowly passed over

figures.name: Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf
figures.role: casting vote; chose Uthman

figures.name: Talha ibn Ubayd Allah
figures.role: shura council member

figures.name: Zubayr ibn al-Awwam
figures.role: shura council member

outcome: Uthman's election began a 12-year caliphate marked by both great expansion and growing internal tensions

title: Byzantine attempt to retake Alexandria fails
description: The Byzantines briefly recapture Alexandria but Amr ibn al-As returns and defeats them permanently securing Muslim control of Egypt.

figures.name: Amr ibn al-As
figures.role: returned to command; defeated Byzantine counter-attack

outcome: Last Byzantine attempt to recover Egypt failed; Muslim control became permanent

visual.caption: Mosque of Amr ibn al-As, Fustat (Old Cairo) — first mosque in Africa

title: First Muslim naval raid on Cyprus
description: Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan launches the first major Muslim naval expedition raiding Cyprus and signaling the emergence of Muslim sea power in the Mediterranean.

figures.name: Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan
figures.role: governor; launched the first Muslim naval campaign

outcome: Birth of Muslim naval power; Mediterranean was no longer a Byzantine lake

visual.caption: Walls of Constantinople — withstood the Arab siege 674-678

title: Conquest of Cyprus
description: Muawiya leads a full naval expedition and conquers Cyprus establishing Muslim naval dominance in the eastern Mediterranean.

figures.name: Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan
figures.role: led the full conquest expedition

outcome: Established permanent Muslim naval presence in the eastern Mediterranean

visual.caption: Walls of Constantinople — withstood the Arab siege 674-678

title: Uthman standardises the Quran (Uthmanic codex)
description: Caliph Uthman commissions Zayd ibn Thabit to produce a standard written Quran; copies are sent to major cities and variant manuscripts are destroyed.

figures.name: Uthman ibn Affan
figures.role: Caliph; ordered standardisation

figures.name: Zayd ibn Thabit
figures.role: chief scribe; led committee producing the standard text

quranRef: Al-Hijr 15:9 — 'Indeed, it is We who sent down the reminder and We will be its guardian'
outcome: Single authoritative Quran text preserved across the Muslim world; textual unity maintained for 1400 years

visual.caption: Birmingham Quran manuscript — among the oldest surviving fragments

title: Fall of the Sassanid Empire
description: The last Sassanid emperor Yazdgerd III is killed in Merv ending over four centuries of Persian imperial rule and bringing Iran fully into the Islamic world.

figures.name: Uthman ibn Affan
figures.role: Caliph; presided over the final conquest of Persia

outcome: End of the Sassanid Empire; Persian civilisation began its transformation within Islam

title: Muslim forces reach the Indus River
description: Expeditionary forces under Uthman's caliphate push eastward into Makran and Sindh reaching the Indus Valley for the first time.

figures.name: Uthman ibn Affan
figures.role: Caliph; presided over eastward expansion

outcome: First Muslim contact with the Indian subcontinent; foreshadowed later Umayyad conquest of Sindh

title: Battle of the Masts — first major naval battle
description: The Muslim fleet defeats a massive Byzantine armada off the Lycian coast in the Battle of the Masts establishing Muslim naval supremacy in the Mediterranean.

figures.name: Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan
figures.role: organised the fleet; strategic architect of Muslim naval power

outcome: Byzantine naval supremacy broken; Muslims became the dominant Mediterranean naval power

title: Assassination of Caliph Uthman
description: Rebels from Egypt and Iraq besiege Uthman's house in Medina for weeks before storming it and killing him while he reads the Quran sparking the first civil war.

figures.name: Uthman ibn Affan
figures.role: Caliph; killed while reading the Quran

outcome: Sparked the First Fitna — the Muslim civil war that permanently divided the community

title: Ali ibn Abi Talib becomes fourth Caliph
description: Ali ibn Abi Talib is pledged allegiance as the fourth Caliph in Medina amid the chaos following Uthman's assassination.

figures.name: Ali ibn Abi Talib
figures.role: became fourth Caliph amid crisis

outcome: Last of the Rashidun caliphs; his caliphate would be consumed by civil war

title: Battle of the Camel
description: Ali's forces clash with an army led by Aisha Talha and Zubayr near Basra over the question of punishing Uthman's killers; Ali prevails but the ummah is deeply divided.

figures.name: Ali ibn Abi Talib
figures.role: Caliph; led forces to victory

figures.name: Aisha bint Abi Bakr
figures.role: led opposition; commanded from atop a camel

figures.name: Talha ibn Ubayd Allah
figures.role: opposition leader; killed in battle

figures.name: Zubayr ibn al-Awwam
figures.role: opposition leader; withdrew then killed while retreating

outcome: Ali won but Muslim unity was shattered; first armed conflict between Companions

title: Battle of Siffin and the Arbitration
description: Ali and Muawiya's armies fight to a stalemate at Siffin on the Euphrates; the raising of Qurans on spears forces arbitration which weakens Ali's position.

figures.name: Ali ibn Abi Talib
figures.role: Caliph; accepted arbitration under pressure

figures.name: Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan
figures.role: Syrian governor; challenged Ali's authority

figures.name: Amr ibn al-As
figures.role: Muawiya's negotiator; outmanoeuvred Ali's representative

outcome: Arbitration weakened Ali; Kharijites broke away; Muawiya's claim strengthened

visual.caption: Bosra, Syria — where young Muhammad met the monk Bahira

title: Emergence of the Kharijites
description: A faction of Ali's supporters reject the arbitration at Siffin and break away forming the Kharijites — the first sectarian movement in Islam.

figures.name: Ali ibn Abi Talib
figures.role: Caliph; lost support from the Kharijite faction

outcome: First formal sectarian split in Islam; Kharijites would assassinate Ali and persist for centuries

title: Battle of Nahrawan
description: Ali defeats the Kharijite rebels at Nahrawan near Baghdad but their movement survives and radicalises leading to his eventual assassination.

figures.name: Ali ibn Abi Talib
figures.role: commander; crushed Kharijite rebellion

outcome: Military victory but political failure; surviving Kharijites swore revenge

visual.caption: Taq Kasra — Arch of Ctesiphon, Sassanid capital ruins

title: Assassination of Ali ibn Abi Talib
description: Ali is struck by a poisoned Kharijite sword while praying at the mosque of Kufa; he dies two days later ending the era of the Rashidun Caliphs.

figures.name: Ali ibn Abi Talib
figures.role: Caliph; assassinated by Kharijite Ibn Muljam during prayer

outcome: End of the Rashidun caliphate; Imam Ali's shrine at Najaf became Shia Islam's holiest site

title: Muawiya establishes the Umayyad Caliphate
description: Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan assumes the caliphate and moves the capital from Medina to Damascus founding the Umayyad dynasty that will rule for nearly a century.

figures.name: Mu'awiya I
figures.role: founded the Umayyad dynasty; moved capital to Damascus

outcome: Caliphate became hereditary monarchy; Damascus replaced Medina as the imperial capital

title: Hasan ibn Ali's treaty with Muawiya
description: Hasan ibn Ali cedes the caliphate to Muawiya in a peace treaty to avoid further Muslim bloodshed; the treaty includes conditions for succession after Muawiya's death.

figures.name: Hasan ibn Ali
figures.role: ceded caliphate to preserve Muslim unity

figures.name: Mu'awiya I
figures.role: accepted treaty; promised to honour succession conditions

outcome: Preserved peace but Muawiya later broke succession terms by nominating Yazid

visual.caption: Taq Kasra — Arch of Ctesiphon, Sassanid capital ruins

title: Ali ibn al-Husayn (Zayn al-Abidin) born
description: The son of Husayn ibn Ali and the great-grandson of the Prophet is born; he will become one of the most revered figures in Islamic spirituality and knowledge.

figures.name: Ali ibn al-Husayn
figures.role: born; future fourth Shia Imam and great scholar

figures.name: Husayn ibn Ali
figures.role: father

outcome: Birth of the scholar-imam who would preserve the Prophet's family legacy after Karbala

title: Death of Hasan ibn Ali
description: Hasan ibn Ali the Prophet's grandson dies in Medina; many sources report he was poisoned marking a deepening of the Sunni-Shia schism over legitimate succession.

figures.name: Hasan ibn Ali
figures.role: Prophet's grandson; died, reportedly poisoned

outcome: Removed the main obstacle to Muawiya's plans for hereditary succession

title: Uqba ibn Nafi founds Kairouan
description: The Muslim general Uqba ibn Nafi establishes the city of Kairouan in modern Tunisia creating the base for the Islamisation of North Africa.

figures.name: Mu'awiya I
figures.role: Caliph; directed the North African campaigns

outcome: Founded North Africa's most important Islamic city; base for the conquest of the Maghreb and Spain

visual.caption: Great Mosque of Kairouan, Tunisia (founded 670 CE)

title: First Arab siege of Constantinople begins
description: Muawiya launches an ambitious naval siege of the Byzantine capital Constantinople; the siege lasts several years but ultimately fails against Greek fire defences.

figures.name: Mu'awiya I
figures.role: Caliph; launched the siege seeking to conquer Constantinople

outcome: First major check on Arab expansion; Constantinople would not fall for another 800 years

visual.caption: Walls of Constantinople — withstood the Arab siege 674-678

title: Siege of Constantinople lifted
description: The Muslim fleet withdraws from Constantinople after Byzantine use of Greek fire inflicts devastating losses marking the first major check on Arab expansion.

figures.name: Mu'awiya I
figures.role: Caliph; forced to accept humiliating withdrawal

outcome: Forced Muawiya to sign a 30-year truce; saved Constantinople for Christendom

visual.caption: Walls of Constantinople — withstood the Arab siege 674-678

title: Death of Muawiya — Yazid I becomes Caliph
description: Muawiya dies having secured succession for his son Yazid breaking the precedent of consultative selection and establishing hereditary Umayyad rule.

figures.name: Mu'awiya I
figures.role: died; had secured hereditary succession

figures.name: Yazid I
figures.role: became Caliph through hereditary succession

outcome: Hereditary monarchy established; broke Hasan treaty terms; triggered Husayn's revolt

title: Battle of Karbala — martyrdom of Husayn
description: Husayn ibn Ali and 72 companions are massacred by Yazid's army at Karbala in Iraq; this event becomes the defining tragedy of Shia Islam and a symbol of resistance to tyranny.

figures.name: Husayn ibn Ali
figures.role: grandson of the Prophet; refused allegiance to Yazid; martyred

figures.name: Yazid I
figures.role: Umayyad Caliph; sent the army

figures.name: Ali ibn al-Husayn
figures.role: Husayn's son; sole surviving male; taken prisoner

figures.name: Zaynab bint Ali
figures.role: Husayn's sister; gave defiant speeches in Damascus captivity

outcome: Permanent Sunni-Shia division crystallised; Ashura became annual day of mourning

visual.caption: Imam Husayn Shrine, Karbala, Iraq
visual.credit: Tasnim News, CC BY-SA 4.0, Wikimedia Commons

title: Revolt of Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr in Mecca
description: Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr declares himself Caliph in Mecca and controls the Hijaz Iraq and parts of Egypt challenging Umayyad authority for nearly a decade.

figures.name: Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr
figures.role: declared himself Caliph in Mecca

figures.name: Yazid I
figures.role: Umayyad Caliph; sent army to suppress the revolt

outcome: Created a rival caliphate lasting 9 years; Mecca became centre of anti-Umayyad resistance

visual.caption: Al-Masjid al-Haram and the Kaaba, Mecca

title: Umayyad army attacks Medina — Battle of al-Harra
description: Yazid's forces sack Medina after the city revolts; the three-day pillage horrifies the Muslim world and permanently damages Umayyad legitimacy.

figures.name: Yazid I
figures.role: Caliph; ordered the sack of the Prophet's city

outcome: Sack of the Prophet's city outraged Muslims; permanently damaged Umayyad religious legitimacy

title: First siege of Mecca — Kaaba damaged by fire
description: Umayyad forces besiege Mecca and catapult stones at the Kaaba causing a fire that damages the sacred structure; Yazid dies during the siege.

figures.name: Yazid I
figures.role: Caliph; died during the siege

figures.name: Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr
figures.role: defender; held Mecca against the siege

outcome: Kaaba damaged by catapult fire; Yazid's death ended the siege and plunged Umayyads into crisis

visual.caption: Al-Masjid al-Haram and the Kaaba, Mecca

title: Death of Yazid I — Second Fitna intensifies
description: Yazid I dies after only three years of rule plunging the Umayyad caliphate into a succession crisis and multi-sided civil war.

figures.name: Yazid I
figures.role: Caliph; died after only 3 years

outcome: Second Fitna escalated; Umayyad state nearly collapsed before Marwan I restored order

visual.caption: Bosra, Syria — where young Muhammad met the monk Bahira

title: Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan becomes Caliph
description: Abd al-Malik takes power in Damascus and spends a decade reasserting Umayyad authority over the fractured caliphate through military force and administrative genius.

figures.name: Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan
figures.role: became Caliph; spent decade reunifying the caliphate

outcome: Saved the Umayyad dynasty; created the administrative infrastructure of the first Islamic empire

title: Dome of the Rock completed in Jerusalem
description: Caliph Abd al-Malik completes the Dome of the Rock on the Temple Mount in Jerusalem — the earliest surviving monumental work of Islamic architecture.

figures.name: Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan
figures.role: patron and commissioner of the building

quranRef: Al-Isra 17:1 — the Night Journey to 'the farthest mosque'
sources: Oleg Grabar, The Dome of the Rock
outcome: Birth of Islamic monumental architecture; established the dome and Quranic mosaic as Islamic visual vocabulary

visual.caption: Dome of the Rock, Jerusalem (691 CE)

title: Hajjaj ibn Yusuf defeats Ibn al-Zubayr
description: The ruthless Umayyad general al-Hajjaj besieges Mecca and kills Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr ending the rival caliphate and reunifying the Umayyad state.

figures.name: Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan
figures.role: Caliph; sent Hajjaj to end the rival caliphate

figures.name: Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr
figures.role: rival Caliph; killed after final stand at the Kaaba

outcome: Umayyad state reunified; Second Fitna ended; but Hajjaj's brutality left lasting bitterness

visual.caption: Al-Masjid al-Haram and the Kaaba, Mecca

title: Arabic replaces Greek and Persian as administrative language
description: Abd al-Malik orders the Arabisation of the imperial bureaucracy replacing Greek in Syria and Persian in Iraq with Arabic as the sole language of government.

figures.name: Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan
figures.role: Caliph; ordered Arabisation of administration

outcome: Arabic became the lingua franca of a civilisation stretching from Spain to Central Asia

title: First purely Islamic coinage minted
description: Abd al-Malik introduces gold dinars and silver dirhams bearing Quranic inscriptions replacing Byzantine and Sassanid coins with distinctly Islamic currency.

figures.name: Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan
figures.role: Caliph; introduced purely Islamic coinage

outcome: Created the first Islamic monetary system; coins became vehicles for Quranic proclamation

visual.caption: Umayyad gold dinar — first purely Islamic coinage

title: Al-Hajjaj appointed governor of Iraq
description: The iron-fisted Hajjaj ibn Yusuf is appointed governor of Iraq and the East; he crushes revolts and builds Wasit but is remembered for his brutality.

figures.name: Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan
figures.role: Caliph; appointed Hajjaj to pacify the East

outcome: Hajjaj crushed all opposition but his tyranny became a byword for oppressive rule in Islamic memory

title: Hasan al-Basri emerges as leading scholar of Basra
description: Hasan al-Basri becomes the most influential preacher and scholar of early Islam in Basra; his circle produces the foundations of Islamic theology and early asceticism.

figures.name: Hasan al-Basri
figures.role: scholar-preacher; founded the most important early Islamic intellectual circle

outcome: His students founded Islamic theology (Kalam), grammar, asceticism, and Mu'tazili thought

title: Conquest of Carthage — end of Byzantine North Africa
description: Muslim forces capture Carthage and expel the last Byzantines from North Africa opening the way for the full Islamisation of the Maghreb.

figures.name: Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan
figures.role: Caliph; directed the North African campaigns

outcome: End of Byzantine North Africa; cleared the path for the Muslim conquest of Spain 13 years later

visual.caption: Great Mosque of Kairouan, Tunisia (founded 670 CE)

title: Umayyad desert palaces flourish
description: The Umayyad caliphs build a network of luxurious desert palaces including Qusayr Amra and Mshatta across the Jordanian and Syrian steppe, featuring frescoes, mosaics, and baths that blend Greco-Roman and Sassanid artistic traditions.
sources: Oleg Grabar, Formation of Islamic Art
outcome: Created a unique Umayyad artistic synthesis of classical and Persian traditions

title: Al-Walid I begins Umayyad architectural golden age
description: Caliph al-Walid I launches monumental building projects including expansion of the Prophet's Mosque, the Al-Aqsa Mosque, and the Great Mosque of Damascus.

figures.name: Al-Walid I
figures.role: Caliph; patron of the greatest Umayyad building programme

outcome: Created the masterpieces of Umayyad architecture

title: Great Mosque of Damascus constructed
description: Al-Walid I builds the Umayyad Mosque in Damascus on the site of the Cathedral of John the Baptist, with golden mosaics depicting paradise created by Byzantine craftsmen.

figures.name: Al-Walid I
figures.role: patron; commissioned Byzantine mosaicists

outcome: Prototype of congregational mosque architecture; mosaics among the finest Islamic art

title: Musa ibn Nusayr conquers western Maghreb
description: The Umayyad governor Musa ibn Nusayr completes the conquest of the western Maghreb, subduing Berber resistance and establishing Muslim control from Egypt to the Atlantic.
outcome: Secured the base for the invasion of Iberia the following year

title: Tariq ibn Ziyad crosses into Iberia
description: Berber commander Tariq ibn Ziyad crosses Gibraltar with 7,000 troops, defeats Visigothic King Roderic at Guadalete, and begins the Muslim conquest of the Iberian Peninsula.

figures.name: Tariq ibn Ziyad
figures.role: commander; defeated Roderic at Guadalete

outcome: Began 800 years of Muslim civilisation in Iberia; Gibraltar = Jabal Tariq

title: Muhammad ibn Qasim conquers Sindh
description: The 17-year-old Umayyad general conquers Sindh, defeating Raja Dahir and establishing the first permanent Muslim rule in South Asia.

figures.name: Muhammad ibn Qasim
figures.role: 17-year-old commander; conquered Sindh

outcome: First permanent Muslim foothold in South Asia

title: Muslims conquer Samarkand and Bukhara
description: Qutayba ibn Muslim conquers the great Central Asian cities of Samarkand and Bukhara, bringing the Silk Road heartland under Umayyad control.
outcome: Brought Central Asia's intellectual and commercial centres into the Islamic world

title: Second Arab siege of Constantinople
description: Caliph Sulayman launches a massive siege of Constantinople with 120,000 troops and 1,800 ships; Greek fire and a harsh winter inflict catastrophic losses.
outcome: Second failure to take Constantinople; confirmed the city would remain Christian

title: Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz — the fifth Rashidun Caliph
description: Umar II implements sweeping reforms: equalising taxes between converts and Arabs, ending religious persecution, and governing with such justice that scholars call him the fifth Rashidun.

figures.name: Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz
figures.role: Caliph; implemented fiscal equality and justice reforms

outcome: Brief reign became the gold standard of righteous rule in Sunni political thought

title: Death of Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz
description: The reformist Caliph dies after only three years, possibly poisoned; his reforms are largely reversed.

figures.name: Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz
figures.role: died; possibly poisoned for his egalitarian reforms

outcome: End of the last attempt to reform the Umayyad state from within

title: Hisham ibn Abd al-Malik becomes Caliph
description: Hisham assumes the caliphate and provides the last period of stable Umayyad rule, strengthening the army, reforming taxation, and expanding agriculture through irrigation projects.
outcome: Last effective Umayyad Caliph; his death precipitated the dynasty's collapse

title: Death of Hasan al-Basri
description: The most influential early Islamic scholar dies; his students founded Islamic theology, grammar, asceticism, and the Mu'tazili school.

figures.name: Hasan al-Basri
figures.role: died; greatest first-century Islamic scholar

outcome: His legacy shaped Islamic theology, jurisprudence, and Sufism for all subsequent centuries

title: Wasil ibn Ata founds the Mu'tazila
description: Wasil ibn Ata separates from Hasan al-Basri's circle and establishes the Mu'tazili school of rational theology, which holds that the Quran is created and that reason can determine moral truth.
outcome: Founded the rationalist school that dominated Islamic theology for over a century

title: Battle of Tours/Poitiers
description: Charles Martel's Frankish army defeats the Umayyad force near Tours, marking the furthest Muslim military advance into western Europe.
outcome: Halted Muslim expansion into western Europe; Frankish power consolidated

title: Khalid ibn Barmak enters Umayyad service
description: The Persian Buddhist-turned-Muslim Khalid ibn Barmak enters government service, beginning the rise of the Barmakid family that will dominate Abbasid administration for decades.
outcome: Began the Barmakid dynasty of viziers who shaped Abbasid golden age governance

title: Great Berber Revolt
description: Berber Muslims revolt across North Africa against Arab taxation discrimination, defeating multiple Umayyad armies and ending direct Umayyad control of the Maghreb.
outcome: Broke Umayyad control of North Africa; led to independent Berber Muslim states

title: Zayd ibn Ali's revolt in Kufa
description: Zayd ibn Ali, grandson of Husayn, leads an armed revolt against the Umayyads in Kufa; he is killed in battle, giving rise to the Zaydi branch of Shia Islam.

figures.name: Zayd ibn Ali
figures.role: led revolt; killed; founded Zaydi Shia tradition

outcome: Founded the Zaydi branch of Shia Islam; Zaydi states later ruled Yemen for centuries

title: Umayyad succession crisis begins
description: The assassination of Caliph al-Walid II triggers a cascade of coups among Umayyad factions, fatally weakening the dynasty.
outcome: Umayyad dynasty consumed by internal strife; cleared the path for Abbasid revolution

title: Abu Muslim launches the Abbasid revolution
description: Abu Muslim unfurls the black banners in Merv, rallying Arab settlers, Persian converts, and discontented Muslims against Umayyad rule.

figures.name: Abu Muslim
figures.role: revolutionary commander; launched the uprising in Khurasan

outcome: Began the revolution that destroyed the Umayyad caliphate

title: Battle of the Zab — Umayyads fall
description: Abbasid forces crush the last Umayyad army at the Great Zab River, ending 90 years of Umayyad rule.

figures.name: Abu al-Abbas al-Saffah
figures.role: first Abbasid Caliph; destroyed Umayyad dynasty

outcome: Power shifted from Arab Damascus to cosmopolitan Baghdad

title: Massacre of the Umayyad family
description: Abbasids systematically kill nearly all Umayyad royals; one prince, Abd al-Rahman, escapes to Spain.

figures.name: Abd al-Rahman I
figures.role: sole major survivor; fled to Spain

outcome: Near-extinction of Umayyads; the survivor founded Córdoba's emirate

title: Battle of Talas — paper reaches Islam
description: Abbasid forces defeat a Tang Chinese army at the Talas River; captured Chinese papermakers introduce paper technology to the Muslim world.
outcome: Paper replaced papyrus across the Islamic world, enabling the golden age of scholarship

title: Abu Muslim executed by al-Mansur
description: Caliph al-Mansur executes Abu Muslim, the revolutionary who made the Abbasid victory possible, fearing his immense power and popularity in Khurasan.

figures.name: Al-Mansur
figures.role: Caliph; ordered execution of the man who won him the throne

outcome: Eliminated the only figure who could challenge Abbasid authority; sparked revolts in Khurasan

title: Abd al-Rahman I founds Córdoba emirate
description: The lone Umayyad survivor reaches al-Andalus, defeats the Abbasid governor, and establishes an independent emirate that will rival Baghdad.

figures.name: Abd al-Rahman I
figures.role: founded Umayyad Córdoba after fleeing the massacre

outcome: Independent Muslim civilisation in Iberia lasting 750 years

title: Baghdad founded as Abbasid capital
description: Caliph al-Mansur founds the Round City of Baghdad at the crossroads of trade routes; it rapidly becomes the world's largest and most cosmopolitan city.

figures.name: Al-Mansur
figures.role: Caliph; designed Baghdad as a circular planned city

outcome: Created the intellectual capital of the world for 500 years

title: Imam Malik begins teaching in Medina
description: Malik ibn Anas establishes his circle at the Prophet's Mosque in Medina, teaching hadith and jurisprudence that will form the basis of the Maliki school.

figures.name: Imam Malik ibn Anas
figures.role: began teaching the Muwatta in Medina

outcome: Founded the Maliki school — dominant in North and West Africa

title: Death of Ja'far al-Sadiq
description: The sixth Shia Imam dies; teacher of both Abu Hanifa and Malik ibn Anas, his students founded the Hanafi, Maliki, and Ja'fari schools.

figures.name: Ja'far al-Sadiq
figures.role: died; teacher of major Sunni and Shia jurists

outcome: Rare figure revered equally by Sunni and Shia; bridged both traditions

title: Death of Abu Hanifa
description: Founder of the Hanafi school dies in Baghdad, reportedly imprisoned for refusing a government post.

figures.name: Abu Hanifa
figures.role: died; founder of the largest Islamic legal school

outcome: Hanafi school became dominant in Turkey, South Asia, and Central Asia

title: Ibn Ishaq completes the Sirah
description: Muhammad ibn Ishaq completes his biography of the Prophet, the earliest surviving Sirah, which becomes the foundational source for all later biographies.

figures.name: Ibn Ishaq
figures.role: author; completed the first comprehensive Prophet's biography

sources: Ibn Ishaq, Sirat Rasul Allah (via Ibn Hisham)
outcome: Created the foundational text of Prophetic biography used by all later historians

title: Jabir ibn Hayyan pioneers experimental alchemy
description: Jabir ibn Hayyan (Geber), working under Ja'far al-Sadiq's intellectual legacy, develops systematic experimental methods in chemistry including distillation, crystallisation, and the classification of substances.

figures.name: Jabir ibn Hayyan
figures.role: pioneer; developed systematic experimental chemistry methods

outcome: Founded the experimental tradition in chemistry; Latin translations shaped European alchemy

title: Indian astronomical texts arrive in Baghdad
description: An Indian scholar brings the Siddhanta astronomical tables to the court of al-Mansur, sparking the Arabic translation of Indian mathematics including the decimal system and zero.
outcome: Introduced Indian numerals and zero to the Islamic world; later transmitted to Europe as 'Arabic numerals'

title: Al-Khwarizmi born — father of algebra
description: Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi is born in Khwarezm; he will write the book that gives algebra its name and introduce the Hindu-Arabic numeral system to the world.

figures.name: Al-Khwarizmi
figures.role: born; future father of algebra and algorithms

outcome: His name gave us 'algorithm'; his book gave us 'algebra'

visual.caption: City walls of Khiva, Central Asia

title: Great Mosque of Córdoba begun
description: Abd al-Rahman I begins construction of the Great Mosque of Córdoba with its forest of double-arched columns.

figures.name: Abd al-Rahman I
figures.role: patron; began the Córdoba mosque

outcome: One of the masterpieces of world architecture; symbol of Andalusian civilisation

title: Harun al-Rashid becomes Caliph
description: Harun al-Rashid begins the most celebrated reign in Islamic history: wealth, scholarship, diplomacy with Charlemagne, and the splendour of the Thousand and One Nights.

figures.name: Harun al-Rashid
figures.role: became Caliph; presided over Baghdad's golden age

outcome: Peak of Abbasid civilisation; Baghdad became the richest city on Earth

title: Barmakid family dominates Abbasid administration
description: The Persian Barmakid family, led by Yahya ibn Khalid and his sons Ja'far and al-Fadl, effectively run the Abbasid empire as viziers under Harun al-Rashid.
outcome: Model of Persian-Arab administrative synthesis; their fall became a legendary tale

title: Idris I founds the Idrisid dynasty in Morocco
description: Idris ibn Abdallah, a descendant of Ali and Hasan, escapes the Abbasid massacre of Alids and founds the first Shia-origin dynasty in Morocco, establishing Fez as his capital.
outcome: First independent Muslim dynasty in Morocco; founded Fez, future intellectual capital

title: Harun al-Rashid exchanges gifts with Charlemagne
description: Harun sends Charlemagne an elephant named Abul-Abbas, a water clock, and silk textiles, establishing the first diplomatic relationship between the Islamic caliphate and the Carolingian empire.
outcome: First direct diplomacy between the Islamic and Carolingian worlds

title: Death of Malik ibn Anas
description: Malik ibn Anas, author of al-Muwatta and founder of the Maliki school, dies in Medina after decades as the city's foremost jurist.

figures.name: Imam Malik ibn Anas
figures.role: died; founder of the Maliki school

outcome: Maliki school became dominant in North Africa, West Africa, and parts of the Gulf

title: Harun al-Rashid destroys the Barmakids
description: Harun suddenly arrests and executes his vizier Ja'far al-Barmaki and imprisons the entire Barmakid family, ending decades of their dominance — one of the most dramatic falls in Islamic history.
outcome: Ended the Barmakid golden era; the fall became a proverb for the fickleness of power

title: Aghlabid dynasty established in Ifriqiya
description: The Abbasids appoint Ibrahim ibn al-Aghlab as hereditary governor of Ifriqiya (Tunisia), founding the Aghlabid dynasty that will conquer Sicily and patronise scholarship.
outcome: Founded the dynasty that conquered Sicily and built Kairouan's golden age

title: Harun al-Rashid at the height of his power
description: Baghdad under Harun al-Rashid is the wealthiest and most cultured city on Earth with a population approaching one million, surpassing Constantinople and dwarfing any European city.
outcome: Baghdad's population and wealth exceeded anything in the contemporary world

title: Fall of the Barmakids complete
description: The once-mighty Barmakid family is destroyed: Ja'far executed, Yahya imprisoned, their vast fortune confiscated. The fall becomes a proverbial tale of power's fickleness.
outcome: Ended the most effective administrative dynasty of the Abbasid era

title: Al-Shafi'i develops systematic legal theory
description: Muhammad ibn Idris al-Shafi'i writes al-Risala, the first systematic work on Islamic legal methodology (usul al-fiqh), establishing the four sources of law: Quran, Sunnah, ijma, and qiyas.

figures.name: Imam al-Shafi'i
figures.role: author; created systematic Islamic legal theory

outcome: Created the science of Islamic legal theory that all subsequent schools follow

title: Death of Harun al-Rashid
description: Harun al-Rashid dies on campaign in Khurasan; his death triggers a civil war between his sons al-Amin and al-Ma'mun that devastates Baghdad.
outcome: His death split the empire between his Arab-backed and Persian-backed sons

title: Al-Ma'mun wins civil war — siege of Baghdad
description: Al-Ma'mun's general Tahir ibn Husayn besieges Baghdad for over a year; al-Amin is killed, and al-Ma'mun becomes Caliph from his base in Merv before eventually moving to Baghdad.
outcome: Baghdad devastated by siege; al-Ma'mun became the most intellectually ambitious Caliph

title: Al-Ma'mun establishes the Bayt al-Hikma
description: Caliph al-Ma'mun formally establishes the House of Wisdom (Bayt al-Hikma) in Baghdad as a major centre for translating Greek, Persian, Sanskrit, and Syriac texts into Arabic.
sources: Dimitri Gutas, Greek Thought, Arabic Culture
outcome: Launched the greatest translation movement in history; preserved Greek philosophy for humanity

title: Al-Khwarizmi writes Kitab al-Jabr
description: Al-Khwarizmi publishes The Compendious Book on Calculation by Completion and Balancing, the founding text of algebra, dedicated to Caliph al-Ma'mun.

figures.name: Al-Khwarizmi
figures.role: author; wrote the book that named algebra

outcome: Gave algebra its name; his work on Hindu numerals gave us 'algorithm'

title: Hunayn ibn Ishaq leads the translation movement
description: The Nestorian Christian scholar Hunayn ibn Ishaq becomes the chief translator at the House of Wisdom, systematically translating Galen, Hippocrates, Plato, and Aristotle into Arabic.
outcome: Translated the entire Greek medical and philosophical corpus into Arabic

title: Al-Ma'mun imposes Mu'tazili doctrine as state creed
description: Caliph al-Ma'mun declares the Mu'tazili rationalist position — that the Quran is created, not eternal — as official state doctrine, launching the Mihna (inquisition) against dissenters.
outcome: Began the Mihna inquisition; Ahmad ibn Hanbal's resistance became legendary

title: Ahmad ibn Hanbal defies the Mihna
description: Ahmad ibn Hanbal refuses to accept the Mu'tazili creed under interrogation and imprisonment, enduring flogging and years of detention rather than compromise his belief in the uncreated Quran.

figures.name: Ahmad ibn Hanbal
figures.role: scholar; defied the Mihna inquisition, endured imprisonment and flogging

outcome: His resistance made him a hero of Sunni orthodoxy; Hanbali school founded on his legacy

title: Palermo falls to Aghlabid Muslims
description: The Aghlabid dynasty completes the conquest of Palermo, making it the capital of Muslim Sicily; the island becomes a centre of Arab-Norman cultural synthesis.
sources: Amari, Storia dei Musulmani di Sicilia
outcome: Muslim Sicily became a model of multicultural coexistence under Arab, then Norman rule

title: Death of al-Ma'mun
description: The most intellectually engaged Caliph dies on campaign against Byzantium; his legacy is the House of Wisdom and the translation movement that preserved Greek learning.
outcome: Left behind the greatest intellectual institution of the medieval world

title: Samarra founded as new Abbasid capital
description: Caliph al-Mu'tasim moves the capital from Baghdad to the new city of Samarra on the Tigris to house his Turkish slave-soldier army away from Baghdad's hostile population.
outcome: Created a new capital with monumental architecture including the famous spiral minaret

title: Sack of Amorion — last great Abbasid offensive
description: Caliph al-Mu'tasim leads 200,000 troops to sack the Byzantine city of Amorion in Anatolia, avenging the Byzantine sack of Zapetra; it is the last great Abbasid military victory against Byzantium.
outcome: Last major Abbasid offensive victory; the empire would turn inward thereafter

title: Al-Kindi writes On First Philosophy
description: Ya'qub al-Kindi, the 'Philosopher of the Arabs,' writes On First Philosophy, the first systematic work of Islamic philosophy, reconciling Greek rationalism with Islamic revelation.

figures.name: Al-Kindi
figures.role: author; first Arab philosopher to systematise Greek thought in Islamic terms

outcome: Founded the Islamic philosophical tradition that led to Ibn Sina and Ibn Rushd

title: Mihna ends — traditionalist Islam triumphs
description: The Abbasid Caliph al-Mutawakkil formally ends the Mihna inquisition, abandoning the Mu'tazili state creed and restoring the traditionalist position that the Quran is uncreated.
outcome: Sunni orthodoxy established permanently; Ahmad ibn Hanbal vindicated

title: Al-Bukhari completes his Sahih
description: Muhammad al-Bukhari completes Sahih al-Bukhari, selecting 7,275 hadith from over 600,000 examined, creating the work universally regarded as the most authentic collection of prophetic traditions.

figures.name: Al-Bukhari
figures.role: author; compiled the most authoritative hadith collection

outcome: Created the gold standard of hadith authentication; most trusted book after the Quran in Sunni Islam

title: Death of Ahmad ibn Hanbal
description: Ahmad ibn Hanbal, hero of the Mihna and founder of the Hanbali school, dies in Baghdad; his funeral is attended by hundreds of thousands.

figures.name: Ahmad ibn Hanbal
figures.role: died; his funeral drew one of the largest gatherings in Baghdad's history

outcome: Founded the Hanbali school; his legacy shaped Sunni orthodoxy, later influencing Wahhabism

title: University of al-Qarawiyyin founded in Fez
description: Fatima al-Fihri, a woman from Kairouan, founds the University of al-Qarawiyyin in Fez, Morocco — recognised by UNESCO and Guinness as the world's oldest continuously operating university.

figures.name: Fatima al-Fihri
figures.role: founder; established the world's oldest university

outcome: World's oldest continuously operating university; centre of Islamic scholarship for over 1,000 years

title: Assassination of Caliph al-Mutawakkil by Turkish guards
description: Turkish military commanders assassinate Caliph al-Mutawakkil in Samarra, beginning an era where Turkish slave-soldiers control the caliphate and make or break Caliphs at will.
outcome: Began the 'Anarchy at Samarra'; Caliphs became puppets of their own military

title: Ahmad ibn Tulun founds the Tulunid dynasty in Egypt
description: The Turkish commander Ahmad ibn Tulun declares effective independence from the Abbasids and founds the Tulunid dynasty in Egypt, building a magnificent new capital and mosque.
outcome: First independent Muslim dynasty in Egypt since the conquest; model for later Fatimid state

title: Al-Tirmidhi and Abu Dawud complete hadith collections
description: Two of the six canonical Sunni hadith collections are completed: Sunan al-Tirmidhi and Sunan Abu Dawud, rounding out the 'Six Books' alongside Bukhari and Muslim.
outcome: Completed the canonical hadith corpus that forms the basis of Sunni legal and theological reasoning

title: Twelfth Imam goes into occultation — Shia theology transformed
description: According to Twelver Shia belief, the twelfth Imam Muhammad al-Mahdi goes into occultation (ghayba) at age five, beginning the Lesser Occultation and fundamentally shaping Shia eschatology.
outcome: Defined Twelver Shia theology and eschatology; the awaited Mahdi remains central to Shia belief

title: Zanj Revolt threatens Abbasid heartland
description: A massive revolt of East African slaves (Zanj) in southern Iraq under Ali ibn Muhammad establishes an independent state for 14 years, sacking Basra and threatening Baghdad.
outcome: Largest slave revolt in Islamic history; devastated southern Iraq's agricultural economy

title: Ibn Tulun Mosque completed in Cairo
description: Ahmad ibn Tulun completes his grand mosque in al-Qata'i (Cairo), with its distinctive spiral minaret inspired by the Malwiya of Samarra — one of the oldest intact mosques in Egypt.
outcome: One of Egypt's greatest architectural monuments; oldest intact mosque in Cairo

title: Al-Farabi born — Second Teacher after Aristotle
description: Abu Nasr al-Farabi is born in Turkestan; he will be called 'the Second Teacher' (after Aristotle) for his synthesis of Greek philosophy with Islamic thought.

figures.name: Al-Farabi
figures.role: born; future 'Second Teacher' of Islamic philosophy

outcome: His political philosophy and logic shaped all subsequent Islamic and European thought

title: Samanid dynasty rises as champions of Persian culture
description: The Samanid dynasty consolidates power over Transoxiana and Khurasan from their capital Bukhara, becoming the great patrons of Persian literature and the New Persian language.
outcome: Launched the Persian literary renaissance; their court produced Firdawsi's Shahnameh tradition

title: Al-Tabari begins writing his universal history
description: Muhammad ibn Jarir al-Tabari begins composing his monumental Tarikh al-Rusul wa al-Muluk (History of Prophets and Kings), the most comprehensive work of Islamic historiography ever written.

figures.name: Al-Tabari
figures.role: author; wrote the definitive Islamic universal history

outcome: Created the foundational text of Islamic historiography used by all subsequent historians

title: Abbasid capital returns to Baghdad
description: After nearly 60 years in Samarra, the Abbasid court permanently returns to Baghdad under Caliph al-Mu'tadid, though by now the Caliphs control little beyond central Iraq.
outcome: Baghdad regained its status as capital, but the Abbasid empire had fragmented irreversibly

title: Ismaili Shia missionary network spreads across Muslim world
description: The Ismaili da'wa (missionary network) operates secretly from Yemen to North Africa, preparing the ground for the Fatimid revolution that will create a rival caliphate.
outcome: Laid the groundwork for the Fatimid caliphate — Islam's most successful revolutionary movement

title: Samanids patronise New Persian literature
description: Under Samanid patronage, the New Persian literary tradition flourishes; Rudaki, the 'father of Persian poetry,' composes at the Bukhara court, and Bal'ami translates Tabari's history into Persian.
outcome: Birth of the Persian literary tradition that produced Firdawsi, Rumi, Hafez

title: Fatimid Caliphate established in North Africa
description: The Ismaili Shia imam Ubayd Allah al-Mahdi overthrows the Aghlabids and establishes the Fatimid Caliphate in Ifriqiya, creating a rival to the Abbasid caliphate.

figures.name: Ubayd Allah al-Mahdi
figures.role: first Fatimid Caliph; overthrew Aghlabids

outcome: Created the only Shia caliphate in history; rival to both Abbasids and Umayyads of Córdoba

title: Abd al-Rahman III becomes Emir of Córdoba
description: The greatest Umayyad ruler of al-Andalus begins his 49-year reign, transforming Córdoba into the most advanced city in Europe.
outcome: Began the golden age of al-Andalus; Córdoba rivalled Baghdad and Constantinople

title: Execution of al-Hallaj in Baghdad
description: The mystic Husayn ibn Mansur al-Hallaj is executed in Baghdad for declaring 'Ana al-Haqq' (I am the Truth/God), becoming the most famous martyr of Sufism.

figures.name: Al-Hallaj
figures.role: Sufi mystic; executed for his ecstatic declaration 'I am the Truth'

outcome: Became Sufism's most famous martyr; his death defined the tension between mysticism and orthodoxy

title: Abd al-Rahman III declares himself Caliph
description: The Umayyad emir of Córdoba takes the title of Caliph, creating a third caliphate alongside the Abbasids and Fatimids — the Islamic world now has three rival Caliphs.
outcome: Three simultaneous caliphates; Córdoba declared equal to Baghdad and the Fatimid capital

title: Buyid dynasty captures Baghdad
description: The Shia Iranian Buyid dynasty captures Baghdad and reduces the Abbasid Caliph to a puppet; for the first time, a Shia dynasty controls the Sunni caliphate's capital.
outcome: Abbasid Caliphs became powerless figureheads; real power held by Buyid military commanders

title: Al-Ash'ari founds orthodox Sunni theology
description: Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari, a former Mu'tazili, develops a middle path between rationalism and literalism that becomes the dominant school of Sunni theological thought.

figures.name: Al-Ash'ari
figures.role: founder; created the dominant school of Sunni theology

outcome: Ash'ari theology became the mainstream of Sunni Islam, followed by the majority to this day

title: Al-Farabi writes The Virtuous City
description: Al-Farabi completes his masterwork on political philosophy, combining Plato's Republic with Islamic political thought to envision the ideal Islamic state.

figures.name: Al-Farabi
figures.role: author; 'Second Teacher' after Aristotle

sources: Al-Farabi, Kitab Ara Ahl al-Madina al-Fadila
outcome: Created Islamic political philosophy; influenced Ibn Sina, Maimonides, and Aquinas

title: Al-Mutanabbi — greatest Arabic poet — flourishes
description: Ahmad ibn al-Husayn al-Mutanabbi, universally regarded as the greatest Arabic poet, composes his masterworks at the court of Sayf al-Dawla in Aleppo.

figures.name: Al-Mutanabbi
figures.role: poet; composed at Sayf al-Dawla's court in Aleppo

outcome: Considered the greatest Arabic poet; his verse is still memorised and quoted across the Arab world

title: Al-Mas'udi writes Meadows of Gold
description: Ali al-Mas'udi completes Muruj al-Dhahab (Meadows of Gold), an encyclopedic work combining history, geography, and natural science — he is later called the 'Herodotus of the Arabs'.

figures.name: Al-Mas'udi
figures.role: author; 'Herodotus of the Arabs'

outcome: Created a model of universal history combining science, geography, and culture

title: Conversion of the Karakhanids — Islam enters the Turkic steppe
description: The Karakhanid ruler Satuq Bughra Khan converts to Islam, bringing the first major Turkic dynasty into the Islamic world and opening Central Asia to Turkish-Islamic civilisation.
outcome: First mass Turkic conversion to Islam; opened the path for the Seljuks, Ottomans, and Mughals

title: Fatimids conquer Egypt and found Cairo
description: The Fatimid general Jawhar al-Siqilli conquers Egypt and founds the new city of al-Qahira (Cairo) as the Fatimid capital, shifting the dynasty's power base from Tunisia to the Nile.
outcome: Founded Cairo — one of the greatest cities in Islamic history; Egypt became the Fatimid heartland

title: Al-Azhar Mosque founded in Cairo
description: The Fatimids found al-Azhar Mosque in Cairo, which becomes al-Azhar University — the oldest degree-granting university in the world and the foremost centre of Sunni learning to this day.
outcome: Founded the institution that remains the world's foremost centre of Islamic scholarship

title: Al-Azhar becomes a university
description: Al-Azhar transitions from a mosque to a full university with regular courses in Islamic law, theology, logic, grammar, and the sciences — the oldest degree-granting university in the world.
sources: Dodge, Al-Azhar: A Millennium of Muslim Learning
outcome: World's oldest degree-granting university; remains the supreme authority in Sunni scholarship

title: Al-Aziz billah expands Fatimid empire
description: Fatimid Caliph al-Aziz expands the empire to include Syria and the Hijaz, making the Fatimids the most powerful Muslim dynasty controlling Mecca, Medina, Jerusalem, and Cairo.
outcome: Fatimids at their peak: controlled the three holiest cities of Islam simultaneously

title: Ibn Sina born — greatest Islamic polymath
description: Abu Ali ibn Sina (Avicenna) is born near Bukhara; he will write the Canon of Medicine and the Book of Healing, shaping both Islamic and European science for centuries.

figures.name: Ibn Sina
figures.role: born; future author of the Canon of Medicine

outcome: His Canon was the standard medical textbook in Europe until the 17th century

title: Al-Muqaddasi writes the best geographical survey
description: Muhammad al-Muqaddasi completes Ahsan al-Taqasim, the finest medieval geographical work, based on his personal travels across every region of the Islamic world.

figures.name: Al-Muqaddasi
figures.role: author; personally traveled every region he described

sources: Al-Muqaddasi, Ahsan al-Taqasim fi Ma'rifat al-Aqalim
outcome: Created the most accurate and detailed geography of the medieval Islamic world

title: Al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah becomes Fatimid Caliph
description: The eccentric and controversial al-Hakim becomes Fatimid Caliph at age 11, beginning a reign marked by both brilliant patronage of science and erratic persecution.
outcome: His mysterious disappearance in 1021 gave rise to the Druze faith

title: Mahmud of Ghazna begins conquests into India
description: Sultan Mahmud of Ghazna launches the first of his seventeen campaigns into the Indian subcontinent, establishing Ghaznavid rule and bringing Islam deep into South Asia.
outcome: Began the systematic spread of Islam into the Indian subcontinent

title: Firdawsi completes the Shahnameh
description: Abu al-Qasim Firdawsi completes the Shahnameh (Book of Kings), the Persian national epic of 50,000 couplets recounting Iran's pre-Islamic history — the longest poem by a single author.

figures.name: Firdowsi
figures.role: poet; completed the 50,000-couplet Persian national epic

outcome: Preserved Persian identity within Islam; the greatest literary work in the Persian language
